In this blog post, I'm covering:

  • Analytics Enhancements
  • Carbon Balance for Production Processes
  • Expanded Gas Impact Categories for Environmental Footprint Analysis
  • Footprint Data Offloading
  • Emission Factors: Expanded Default Content & Sustamize Partnership
  • Default Content from ADEME and EPA
  • Partner Content from Sustamize
  • Other Release Highlights
  • Outlook: Footprint Optimization Agent & CBAM Solution
  • Footprint Optimization Agent
  • CBAM Solution
  • Analyst Recognition:IDC MarketScape Leader
  • Additional Information

 

Analytics Enhancements

Good analytics are what turn footprint calculations into decisions, so this quarter we focused on the Footprint Overview app, which now includes new functions for an improved user experience. The updated app aligns with sustainability reporting frameworks and introduces footprint comparison as a capability, so you can put results side by side instead of looking at them in isolation. In addition, the top five contributors are now directly available on the main view, helping you act on insights instead of only validating calculation results.

The app organizes results into four sections – Climate, Products, Energy, and Waste – accessible from a navigation panel. The Climate section shows total GHG emissions broken down by GHG scope and category, and surfaces the top five plants by emissions; the Energy, Products, and Waste sections highlight the most significant contributors among energy sources and consumers, purchased and sold products, and waste in operations. From any chart you can drill into the underlying records in apps such as GHG Emissions, Manage Footprint Results, or Waste Generated in Operations. The new Compare With option lets you benchmark a selected inventory against a reference footprint inventory, calculation variant, or period, showing primary and reference values side by side together with the absolute and percentage difference. With comparison and the top contributors surfaced up front, it becomes much faster to spot where your biggest hotspots are and how they shift between periods or scenarios. For more information, see Analyzing Footprint Overview on SAP Help.

 

 

Carbon Balance for Production Processes

One of the key innovations this quarter is the new carbon balance calculation for production processes. With this feature, SAP Sustainability Footprint Management enables you to track and calculate direct Scope 1 – Process Emissions from production by comparing the carbon entering a process with the carbon leaving it. The approach is modeled on the principle of mass balance, such as the methodology outlined in the EU Emissions Trading System (ETS) Monitoring and Reporting Regulation, so the resulting emissions are grounded in an established regulatory accounting method.

This is especially relevant for production processes where carbon is physically transformed. To use the new approach, you provide carbon content data for the production-related business transactions that represent the two sides of the balance. Goods Issue for Production represents material issued from a plant with reference to a production document, while Goods Receipt from Production represents material received at a plant with reference to a production document.

When you calculate the production footprint, the system first determines the difference in carbon content by subtracting the sum of the output carbon content from the sum of the input carbon content – the amount of carbon directly emitted during the production step. This difference is then multiplied by the C-to-CO2 conversion factor to translate it into Climate Change CO2e, which is added to the total footprint of the production and distributed to the output items. The result can be either positive or negative depending on the relationship between input and output carbon: if more carbon is emitted than is captured in the outgoing products, the value is negative, whereas if more carbon is captured in the outputs, the value is positive. Where production is not yet closed, work-in-progress items carry their carbon content over into the next period.

After a successful calculation, you can view the production results in the Manage Footprint Results app. For footprint items of type Production, the carbon balance appears as an input item under the Allocation category – connected to the business transaction Allocation on Production – and its climate change impact is shown in the Sankey chart quick view. The underlying moisture and carbon content values for the in-going and out-going products are available on the Input and Output tabs for review. Under Allocation, you can also navigate to the calculation details and formula of the carbon balance.

 

To support this capability, the Import Business Transactions (Corporate and Products) API has also been enhanced with the new moistureContent and carbonContentPerUnit fields. For more information, see Calculating Carbon Balance and the scenario guide Calculating Scope 1 Emissions (Carbon Content) Using Business Transaction Data on SAP Help.

 

Expanded Gas Impact Categories for Environmental Footprint Analysis

In previous updates, I introduced how SAP Sustainability Footprint Management is evolving from a carbon-focused solution into a broader environmental footprint platform (see: Deep Dive: Multi-Impact Categories in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management). This quarter, we’re continuing that journey with seven new gas-related impact categories for environmental footprint calculations:

  • CO2 – Carbon Dioxide (Total Biogenic and Fossil)
  • CH4 – Methane (Total Biogenic and Fossil)
  • N2O – Nitrous Oxide
  • HFCs – Hydrofluorocarbons (Aggregated GHGs)
  • PFCs – Perfluorocarbons (Aggregated GHGs)
  • SF6 – Sulphur Hexafluoride
  • NF3 – Nitrogen Trifluoride

This gives you more transparency into the constituent gases behind the overall greenhouse gas (GHG) picture, with each gas calculated using its own characterization factors based on global warming potential, and separate biogenic and fossil treatment for CO2 and methane. For more information on the gases, see Impact Categories on SAP Help.

In the Manage Emission Factors app, you can maintain emission factors for the new gas categories either directly in the UI or by importing them via the app template, as with the other impact categories.

In the Manage Footprint Inventory Scopes app, you activate the relevant impact categories for your scope. With this release, you can activate up to 12 impact categories, including Climate Change, which remains enabled by default. Once scoped and calculated, the new categories are visible in the Manage Footprint Results app, where the system shows total footprints and statuses across the selected impact categories and the contribution of GHG categories into them.

Constituent Gases in the Item Footprint

 

Footprint Data Offloading

As customers run footprint calculations period after period, the volume of calculated inventory data grows over time. To help you manage that data lifecycle, this quarter we introduced the new Manage Footprint Data Offloads app, which lets you move calculated footprint inventories to long-term storage once they are no longer needed in the active system. This helps you optimize system performance, reduce license consumption, and support your organizational sustainability objectives, all while keeping the data available for the duration you need it.

To make this controllable, footprint data offloading is built around two configurable time periods that you now set per scope in the Manage Footprint Inventory Scopes app:

  • The residence period is the minimum time that calculated footprint inventory data stays in the system before it becomes eligible for offloading to long-term storage. It must be at least 24 months and is part of the overall retention period.
  • The retention period is the total time the data must be kept, including both the residence period in the system and the time in long-term storage. It must also be at least 24 months. After the retention period ends, you can permanently delete the data.

The Manage Footprint Data Offloads app lists all calculated footprint inventories with the status Calculated Successfully, Calculated with Errors, or Calculated. Once an inventory’s residence period has ended, you can offload it from this app, and you can also delete the data of calculated footprint inventories to manage your data lifecycle and system resources. Offloaded inventories can be downloaded as JSON files, so the data remains accessible for archiving, reporting, or audit purposes after it leaves the active system. Offloading actions are also captured in the audit logs.

Manage Footprint Data Offloads

If you’re already a customer, this transition is handled for you: any footprint inventories created before this feature are automatically migrated, with the residence period set to 24 months and the retention period set to 36 months by default. You can adjust these periods per scope going forward to match your own data governance and reporting requirements. Note that to use the app, you need the relevant footprint data and calculation roles. For more information, see Offloading Calculated Footprint Inventories in the Release Impact Information on SAP Help.

 

Emission Factors: Expanded Default Content & Sustamize Partnership

High-quality emission factors remain the foundation for accurate and audit-ready footprint results. This quarter, we broadened the emission factor content from two complementary angles: we refreshed the default content and we added new premium partner content that you can purchase through the SAP Store. As always, the data flows into the Manage Emission Factors app, so you can mix SAP-provided content, partner content, and your own imported data packages within one consistent governance and calculation model.

Default Content from ADEME and EPA

Also this quarter, we further extended the default emission factor content that SAP Sustainability Footprint Management delivers out of the box. We’ve added emission factors from the French Agency for Ecological Transition (ADEME), which is the official public reference database of emission factors for France, organized along the GHG Protocol’s Scope 1, 2, and 3 structure and used as the basis for regulatory greenhouse gas reporting in the French market. Adding ADEME as default content gives you well-recognized, France-relevant factors out of the box, which is particularly valuable if you operate in or source from France.

Furthermore, you can now use new versions of emission factors from the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), spanning across the years 2015, 2018, 2020-2022, and 2025. The EPA packages are U.S.-focused and include content for GHG Scopes 1, 2, and 3.

Taken together, this is a substantial expansion of what you get out of the box: all default content draws on six different open-source databases and comprises around 45,300 individual datasets, across UK, US, France, Germany, as well as Europe and Global geographies. The datasets cover Energy, Fuels, Purchased Products, Passenger Travel, Transport of Goods, Process Emissions, and Refrigerants, giving you much wider coverage across categories and regions before you need to bring in your own or partner content. As before, this default content is kept separate from any factors you import yourself: if you don’t maintain your own datasets you can rely on the default content directly, and if you do, you continue to import, release, upgrade, archive, and delete your own data packages alongside it within the same governance model. For more information, see Using Default Emission Factor Data in SAP Help.

Partner Content from Sustamize

Beyond the content SAP delivers directly, we’re also constantly exploring partnership options and I’m happy to share that Sustamize emission factor datasets are now available through the SAP Store: Sustamize Scope 3 Data / Carbon Footprint Data. Sustamize provides high-quality, fully regionalized CO2e emission factors for product carbon footprints and Scope 3 calculations, with over 10 million emission factors across materials, components, and processes. The datasets are continuously updated, validated, and aligned with standards such as the GHG Protocol and ISO 14067, and they integrate directly into SAP Sustainability Footprint Management for scalable, audit-ready emissions calculations.

This partnership means you can:

  • Access a large, granular CO2e database across materials, processes, and regions.
  • Strengthen auditability and compliance with standardized data.
  • Increase accuracy with regional electricity consumption mix data.
  • Enable automated Scope 3 and product carbon footprint (PCF) calculations at scale.
  • Stay current with regularly updated datasets reflecting real-world conditions.

Because this content is consumed through the same Manage Emission Factors app as the rest of your data packages, you can adopt it without changing how you scope, calculate, or govern your footprints.

 

Other Release Highlights

In the Manage Purchased Product Footprints app, you can now enter a plant when creating or importing purchased product group footprints. The plant represents geographical information, such as country or region, so you can define emission factors for a product group at plant level and better capture geographical differences. This works together with the Purchased product footprint on the product group level determination method in the Manage Calculation Variants app, and if both a plant-specific and a non-plant-specific footprint exist for the same product group, the system uses the more specific footprint with plant assignment. See Creating Footprints for Purchased Products and Product Groups on SAP Help for more details.

The Waste Generated in Operations app now displays a subtotal row at the end of each group in the table, showing combined Waste Quantity and Total Footprint values so you can see each group’s total contribution without manually adding individual rows. For more information, see Analyzing Footprints for Waste Generated in Operations on SAP Help.

The Manage Locations app now includes a read-only field showing which address fields were used to calculate geocoordinates. The system first uses all available address fields and, if that returns no result, retries using only country/region and postal code, so minor address issues no longer necessarily prevent a location from receiving accurate geocoordinates. For more details, see Manage Locations on SAP Help.

On the API side, the existing Item Footprints API has been renamed to Item Footprints - V1 and is now deprecated (bug fixes only), while a new Item Footprints - V2 API is available on SAP Business Accelerator Hub with a similar payload structure but improved performance for bulk operations. The batch size capacity for the Item Footprints API has also been expanded from 1,000 to 10,000 records. See APIs for Analytics on SAP Help for all details. In addition, the Import Business Transactions (Corporate and Products) API now includes the new moistureContent and carbonContentPerUnit fields supporting the carbon balance calculation. For more information, see APIs for Data Import on SAP Help.

 

Outlook: Footprint Optimization Agent & CBAM Solution

Beyond the features that shipped this quarter, I also want to share a brief outlook on where we’re heading, across both AI and regulatory topics. For staying updated on all planned innovations, I recommend checking out SAP Road Map Explorer.

Footprint Optimization Agent

At SAP Sapphire this May, SAP announced a new family of sustainability AI agents, planned for general availability by the end of 2026, that embed carbon, compliance, and safety intelligence directly into everyday business workflows rather than treating sustainability as a separate, manual exercise. The line-up spans regulatory readiness, packaging compliance, hazard classification and labeling, workplace safety, and footprint optimization. Read here the full press release: The Path to the Autonomous Enterprise: SAP Announces New Sustainability AI Agents.

The Footprint Optimization Agent is part of SAP Sustainability Footprint Management and is designed to help you move from footprint insights to informed action with real impact. It lets you review your corporate and product carbon footprints faster and in more detail, analyzing emission sources across multiple dimensions – products, processes, plants, and value-chain stages – on a consistent calculation foundation, so you can identify footprint hotspots and the reduction levers that matter most.

From there, you can run what-if scenarios to examine how operational choices such as production methods, energy inputs, and sourcing patterns would affect your footprint, and compare emission-reduction scenarios side by side against your baseline. Because all simulations rely on the same underlying footprint data and shared assumptions, the results are directly comparable, helping you understand whether a change reduces impact and by how much. The agent can then generate executive-ready views of footprint calculations, drivers, scenarios, and comparisons, giving sustainability, operations, and finance teams a shared starting point to prioritize high-impact initiatives, map the financial impact, and track progress against reduction goals. The Footprint Optimization Agent is planned to be generally available in Q4 2026, so I’ll come back to it in more detail once it’s released. For more details, see the business case Reduce Carbon Footprint More Efficiently with AI.

The following video is part of this year's sustainability showcase at SAP Sapphire and gives you a preview of how AI agents automate reporting - creating data points and linking metrics to disclosures with traceable lineage. It shows how you can use these agents to identify carbon hotspots, simulate scenarios, and recommend actions balancing cost and risk (Footprint Optimization Agent starts at 3:22).

 

The Footprint Optimization Agent is however only one part of how we’re bringing AI into the product. We’re also continuing to invest in the AI capabilities already embedded in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management. For the AI-assisted emission factor mapping we plan to release significant improvements in July already to make mapping your products, materials, and business transactions to suitable emission factors faster, more accurate, and easier to govern. Stay tuned for the details.

CBAM Solution

With the CBAM definitive regime having started on January 1, 2026, we also announced new Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) capabilities planned for SAP Sustainability Footprint Management together with SAP Green Ledger later this year. CBAM reporting requires total embedded emissions that are consistent, repeatable, and traceable, often across thousands of shipments and suppliers, which is exactly the kind of scaled calculation SAP Sustainability Footprint Management is built for.

The planned CBAM capabilities in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management will include centralizing ERP and master data enhanced for CBAM purposes alongside official CBAM content, automating the interpretation and use of supplier data, calculating embedded emissions, certificates, and estimated CBAM cost, a CBAM dashboard to view and analyze emissions and costs, and the generation of reports ready for download and submission to the EU. The exact cost calculation will be based on the weekly EU ETS price in SAP Green Ledger, which accounts for the financial liability triggered by CBAM-covered emissions and the certificates used to settle it, in line with IFRS and US GAAP. Together, SAP Sustainability Footprint Management and SAP Green Ledger connect emissions data with the financial dimensions companies already use, so carbon can be governed, allocated, forecasted, audited, and reconciled in line with financial data. The first release is planned for Q4 2026, and I’ll share more details in the next months. To learn more about CBAM and our solution, read the following blog: CBAM Definitive Regime: What to Know and Actions to Take.

 

Analyst Recognition: IDC MarketScape Leader

I’m also happy to share that for the second time SAP has been recognized as a Leader in the IDC MarketScape: Worldwide Carbon Accounting and Management Applications 2026 Vendor Assessment. The assessment evaluated 17 vendors on how well they support credible emissions measurement, strong data governance, corporate-, product-, and supplier-level visibility, and standards-based reporting. It singles out SAP’s ERP-embedded approach, which “unifies financial, operational, and sustainability data into a single, trusted foundation”, as a key strength. SAP Sustainability Footprint Management is a core part of this suite, enabling organizations to calculate carbon footprints across Scopes 1, 2, and 3. It’s great external validation of the direction many of the features in these updates are taking. For more information and downloading the report, see the SAP News Feature: SAP Named a Leader in the IDC MarketScape: Worldwide Carbon Accounting and Management Applications 2026 Vendor Assessment.

 

Additional Information

For in-depth details on SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, including concepts, setup, and the application help, I recommend you browsing through the SAP Help Portal. For an overview of all new features and functions that have been released, check the What’s New section, which is updated with every new software release.

To see what’s ahead, the SAP Road Map Explorer gives you a forward-looking view of planned innovations for SAP Sustainability Footprint Management. And if you’d like to help shape the product, the SAP Influence portal is the place to submit improvement requests and vote on ideas from other customers and partners – many of the enhancements I cover in these posts started as customer requests there.

For exploring and experimenting in a live system, register for the free SAP Sustainability Footprint Management Trial. You’ll receive an e-mail with access information within seconds after your registration and can then test the solution at your own convenience within a 30-day trial period. It also includes a wide range of guided tours designed to help you navigate through the features and functions comprehensively.

To stay up to date on SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, follow me here in the SAP Community. In regular cadence, I’ll publish blog posts giving you updates on all the product’s new features and functions as well as deep dives into the product capabilities. I’m also looking forward to engaging with you directly, feel free to leave your feedback in the comment section.

entry.title: SAP Continuous Influence Session for SAP S/4HANA Utilities - Third Anniversary Update
entry.content:
Three years ago already, we have launched the SAP Continuous Influence session for SAP S/4HANA Utilities as our single channel for collecting your suggestions for improvements related to SAP S/4HANA Utilities:

For background information, please refer to my previous blog post New SAP Continuous Influence session for SAP S/4HANA Utilities.

To mark this milestone, I'm pleased to share a status update on our progress.

Session Statistics (as of 1 July 2026)

In total we have 149 improvement requests (IRs) in our session.

51 IRs delivered – including 23 delivered during the last twelve months, , starting with those that have received most votes:

  • IR 351738 - Add Fields 'Market Location' and 'Metering Location' for App 'Resolve Implausible Meter Readings' 
  • IR 364511 - Request for Better Error Management in IS-U PF Process 
  • IR 357184 - §40 EnWG gesetzliche Verpflichtung zur aktiven Übermittlung einer Verbrauchsübersicht 
  • IR 357107 - Re-plausibilisation for later meter readings (ISU_DM_MR_FUTURE_PLAUSI active) 
  • IR 355596 - RTP-Schnittstelle mit Eingabevariable in Komponente / RTP-Interface with Input variable ... 
  • IR 354708 - Pre-filling of fields during the connection object search by address (Customer Engagement/ IC Agent) 
  • IR 354703 - Additional search options are needed in the free-text search of premise(Customer Engagement/IC Agent 
  • IR 330456 - Enterprise search for utilities objects 
  • IR 316432 - EDM: Customizing of profile version creation is not considered during settlement run 
  • IR 346592 - Fälligkeitsdatum beim Druckbeleg-Storno  
  • IR 373238 - Fiori IS-U Overview App based on a search (e.g Address, Customer, Point of Delivery, Installation) 
  • IR 317134 - EEDM02 transaction in Fiori 
  • IR 328753 - Deleting device info records en masse 
  • IR 345970 - Enhancement of Transaction EEDMPROFVALCHECK 
  • IR 346630 - Transaktion EEDMPROFVALCHECK Option “nur abrechnungsfähige Profilstatus“ 
  • IR 358288 - set due date of budget billing plan during invoicing 
  • IR 348044 - Profile Status and Status of Billing Orders do not correspond  
  • IR 352844 - Felder für Biller Direct sind in Customer Engagement nicht verfügbar 
  • IR 368454 - Allow meter reader (Ableser) in combination with meter reading type „Meter reading by the customer“ 
  • IR 331497 - MDUS Additional Attributes 
  • IR 346808 - Versorgungsanlage im Datenumfeld farblich differenzierbar Request 
  • IR 349323 - Expand utilities sales contract search with POD external ID 
  • IR 357685 - Analyzing Meter Reading Records with Reason Code 09 with Missing Gas Data 

For a complete list of all delivered improvement requests, visit: https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/campaign/3504/ideas-completed

3 IRs accepted and planned to be delivered in Q3–Q4 2026:

  • IR 363172 - Increase the maximum character limit for the “Code for Identifying a Register” 
  • IR 366770 - EG72 Device allocation additional start time and end time/ EG72 Gerätezuordnung zusätzl. Uhrzeit 
  • IR 356178 - Replacement of iMS (+smgw to mME)-Enablement of ami ws for assignment of base meter to MaLo/MeLo 

4 IRs marked as Planned (Long-Term) – identified as larger investments and included in the SAP S/4HANA Utilities product roadmap:

  • IR 330477 - Flexible Aggregation von Lastgängen / Flexible aggregation of load profiles 
  • IR 351296 - Fiori Billing and Metering Reading Overview/Analysis Apps 
  • IR 323237 - Public API for Developer Extensibility: RAP Interface for Utilities Contract Accounts /V... 
  • IR 352899 - SAP Fiori App for Contract Account Creation/Change/Display 

5  IRs under review.

19 IRs with Acknowledged status, including 5 with 7+ votes (approaching the 10-vote threshold for review)

2 IRs requiring additional information.

64 IRs not planned: Most rejected due to insufficient votes within 12 months or being outside the scope of utilities-specific functionality.

Important Reminders

This session focuses exclusively on utilities-industry specific features in SAP S/4HANA. For improvement requests related to other SAP S/4HANA topics, please see How Customers and Partners Can Give Input to the Roadmap of the SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition and the SAP Continuous Influence Sessions overview.

Please note that a valid S user is required to participate in our (and other) SAP Continuous Influence session(s). For details see knowledge base article 3014377 - Accessing the Continuous Influence Session "SAP S/4HANA Utilities". 

Thank You

We appreciate  for your active participation and engagement. Your input helps us to continuously improve SAP S/4HANA Utilities according to your business requirements.

entry.title: Bypassing the List Report in SAP Fiori Apps: BCMO, Static Keys, and Dynamic Keys
entry.content:
If you've ever generated a Business Configuration Maintenance Object (BCMO) app or built a standard List Report Fiori app for a table with just one relevant record, you've probably run into the same annoyance: the app insists on showing you a list page first, even when there's really only one record, or one record per user, that anyone should ever land on.

In this post, I'll walk through the complete flow: generating a BCMO app for a custom table, getting rid of the pointless singleton list report, and then bypassing the list page in a regular List Report app using both a static key and a dynamic key, such as the logged-in username.

What is a BCMO App, and How Does It Relate to Table Maintenance Generator (TMG)?

Before we jump into the how, a quick primer for anyone new to this.

A Table Maintenance Generator (TMG) is the classic SAP GUI way of generating a maintenance screen for a customizing table, using transaction SE11/SE54. You get a generated SM30 style screen where users can create, edit, and delete entries in a table.

BCMO, Business Configuration Maintenance Object, is essentially the RAP and Fiori based evolution of the same idea. Instead of generating an SM30 style dynpro, ADT generates a full RAP based application stack, behavior definition, behavior implementation, service definition, and service binding, for your custom table, and exposes it as a modern Fiori app. You get the same core purpose as TMG, maintaining configuration or master data in a table, but with a UI5 based List Report and Object Page instead of a classic table control screen.

So think of BCMO as: you want the SM30/TMG experience, but as a proper Fiori Elements app, generated automatically from your table.

You can access a BCMO app in two ways:

  • Through the Custom Business Configurations (CBC) app, where all the configuration and settings are automatically applied.
  • As a standalone app, published separately to your Fiori Launchpad or SAP Build Work Zone. In this case, the actual table maintenance appears on the object page, but you also get a singleton list report page in front of it, which is usually unnecessary.

That useless singleton list report is exactly what we're going to eliminate below.

Part 1: Creating a BCMO App

Prerequisites on the Database Table

Before ADT will let you generate a BCMO app, your custom table needs to satisfy a few conditions. If you skip these, generation will fail, and you'll typically see errors pointing you toward three things:

  • The table's delivery class needs to be set to C, since BCMO is meant for customizing tables, not regular application data.
  • Data maintenance needs to be explicitly allowed on the table, via the AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance annotation.
  • Character-like key fields need to be based on a domain, rather than a plain built-in type, so the generator can derive proper value help and type information.

Once these three conditions are met, ADT will happily generate the BCMO stack for you. Here's an example table definition that satisfies all of them:

@EndUserText.label : 'Product Industry Mapping'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #C
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #ALLOWED
define table zsac_t_prod_ind {

  key client          : abap.clnt not null;
  key industry_sector : mbrsh not null;
  unit_of_measure     : meins;
  currency_code       : abap.cuky;

}

Generating the BCMO Objects

Right-click on the table in ADT, click Generate ABAP Repository Objects, select Business Configuration Maintenance Object, and click Next.

Enter the package.

In the Configure Generator step, you decide the naming of the generated objects and which features to enable. For this example, we'll set Transport Selection to No Transport and uncheck "Enable Transport Selection Strip" to keep things simple. Depending on your landscape, you may want this enabled instead.

Here's the full list of objects that get generated for us:

After selecting your transport request, or choosing No Transport, click Finish, and sip a cup of tea while ADT does its thing.

Once generation completes, this is what you get in ADT:

Accessing the BCMO App

You can access your BCMO app in two ways:

  1. Through the Custom Business Configurations (CBC) app, where all the standard settings are already applied for you.
  2. As a standalone app, published separately to your SAP Fiori Launchpad or SAP Build Work Zone.

If you go the standalone route, the actual table maintenance will appear correctly on the object page, but you'll also get an unnecessary singleton list report page in front of it, which is exactly the problem we're solving in the next section.

Now open the service binding, publish it, and test it.

A Note on Authorizations

You may notice that an access control object gets automatically generated as part of BCMO. If the authorization object S_TABU_NAM is not assigned to your user role, you may see no data in the output, or the Edit button may be disabled.

In that case, you have two options:

  • Delete the access control on your root entity, or
  • Assign the required authorizations to your user ID.

While testing this, I also had to comment out the global authorization check in the generated behavior implementation just to see data, due to missing authorizations in my BTP trial account. Obviously, don't do this in a real system, it's purely a local testing workaround.

Part 2: Getting Rid of the Singleton List Report for a BCMO App

Now that our BCMO app is ready, let's get rid of the singleton list report. It doesn't add any value, there's only ever one relevant "list," so why make the user click through it every time?

Step 1: Create a New Project in BAS

Go to SAP Business Application Studio and create a new project from the available templates.

Select SAP Fiori Generator.

Select List Report Page and click Next.

Select the Data Source and Service.

Note: If your system isn't logged in yet, log in first. If you're on an SAP BTP trial account, you'll need to connect BAS to Cloud Foundry before you can see the available Service Bindings.

To do that, open the top search bar and type:

>CF: Login to Cloud Foundry

Log in with your credentials or SSO, then reopen the Data Source and Service Selection step.

Once logged in, you should now be able to see the service. Look for your Service Binding for the BCMO app and select it.

Select the entity.

If you're planning to deploy the app immediately, make sure to check "Add Deployment Configuration" and "Add SAP Fiori Launchpad Configuration." For this demo, I'm keeping both unchecked since we're only focused on getting rid of the list report page, not deployment.

Click Finish, and go have another cup of tea while the project scaffolds.

Step 2: Remove the List Report Route from the Manifest

With the project created in BAS, let's get to the main task.

Open manifest.json under the webapp folder and remove the list report route and its corresponding target.

We do this because the default routing configuration always points the app to the list report as the landing page first. By removing this route and target pair, we prevent the router from ever resolving to the list report on initial load, which sets us up to redirect straight to the object page instead.

Step 3: Auto-Navigate to the Object Page in Component.js

Now go to Component.js under the webapp folder and add the following code so the app routes directly to the object page of our singleton record whenever it loads:

init: function () {
    Component.prototype.init.apply(this, arguments);
    this.getRouter().navTo("ProductIndSingletonObjectPage", { key: "(1)" }, true);
}

Step 4: Test It

Run the following command in the terminal to preview the app:

npm run start

And voilà, the first page that opens is no longer the singleton list report, but the actual object page we wanted users to land on directly.

From here, you can continue with deployment as per your requirement, including Fiori Launchpad configuration and transport.

Part 3: Bypassing the List Report for a Standard List Report App

The technique above works great for a true singleton, a table with essentially one record. But what if you're working with a regular List Report app on a table with multiple records, and you want to skip the list page in favor of a specific object page, based on a static key or a dynamic key like the logged-in user? Let's look at both.

Deploying the Base List Report App

As before, start by creating a new project in SAP BAS or VS Code, selecting SAP Fiori Application, then List Report as the template.

In the template wizard, select your Data Source, System, and Service, then click Next. Select the correct main entity and navigation entity, and choose Yes to automatically add table columns and a section to the object page.

Enter your project attribute details, choose Yes for deployment configuration, click Finish, and wait for the dependencies to install. Don't refresh the page while you see "Installing Dependencies" at the bottom right of VS Code.

Once deployed, remove the singleton list report the same way described above: strip the route and target from the manifest, and add the navTo call in Component.js.

The difference this time is what key you pass into that navTo call.

Option A: Bypassing with a Static Key

If you always want the app to open a specific, known record, hardcode the key directly:

init: function () {
    Component.prototype.init.apply(this, arguments);
    this.getRouter().navTo("TableGroupAllObjectPage", { key: "(1)" }, true);
}

This is the simplest approach and works well when there's genuinely one record everyone should see, similar to the BCMO singleton pattern above, but applied manually to a regular List Report app.

Option B: Bypassing with a Dynamic Key (Logged-in Username)

Often, though, you don't want everyone landing on the same record. You want each user routed to their own record, based on who's logged in. For this, we fetch the current username first, then use it to construct the key dynamically before navigating.

init: function () {
    Component.prototype.init.apply(this, arguments);

    var sUser = sap.ushell.Container
        ? sap.ushell.Container.getUser().getId()
        : "DEFAULT_USER";

    this.getRouter().navTo("TableGroupAllObjectPage", { key: "('" + sUser + "')" }, true);
}

A few things to keep in mind with the dynamic key approach:

  • Make sure the key field in your entity actually corresponds to something like a username, or a field you can reliably derive from the logged-in user, for example, mapped via a custom table or CDS view.
  • If no record exists for that user yet, decide upfront how your app should behave: show an empty object page in create mode, show a "no data" message, or fall back to a default record.
  • Test this thoroughly with multiple user IDs before deploying, since the whole point is that different users see different landing records.

For Multi-Level Apps

If your app has more than two navigation levels, for example, a list report page, then an object page showing a related sub-entity's list, then a further object page for that sub-entity's own details, you'll need to add another navigation node to the page map.

To do this, right-click on the app's folder in BAS and select Show Page Map. You'll see the existing levels, with a plus icon enabled on the deepest Object Page. Click it, select the correct navigation property, and add the new page.

Once your routing is set up correctly, deploy using:

npm run deploy

Wrapping Up

Whether you're working with a BCMO app generated straight from ADT, or a hand-built List Report Fiori app, the pattern for bypassing the list page is fundamentally the same:

  1. Remove the list report's route and target from the manifest.
  2. Add a navTo call in Component.js's init function that routes straight to the object page.
  3. Decide whether that route needs a static key, same record for everyone, or a dynamic key, per-user record, typically derived from the logged-in username.
  4. If you have deeper navigation levels, extend the page map accordingly before deploying.

This small change removes a genuinely pointless click for your end users, and it's one of those RAP and Fiori details that separates a generated-and-shipped app from a genuinely polished one.

If deployment configuration for RAP apps to SAP BTP is something you'd like a refresher on before trying this end to end, it's worth reading up on that step separately once you're ready to move beyond local testing.

entry.title: 【DMLTによるデータマネジメントブログ】Vol.0 - データマネジメントブログの連載を開始します
entry.content:
はじめに

はじめまして。SAP の DMLT（Data Management and Landscape Transformation）チームの Japan メンバーです。

この度、SAP システムに関連するデータマネジメントの各テーマについて、実践的な知識や経験を広くお届けしたいという思いから、SAP Community にてブログ連載を開始することにしました。

SAP システムを取り巻く環境は、近年大きく変化しています。SAP S/4HANA への移行はもとより、移行した後も、M&A・AI の隆盛に伴うデータプライバシーへの継続的な対応、増加し続ける SAP HANA Database の Volume Control、Autonomous Enterprise 対応、AI 活用基盤の構築など、データに関わる課題はますます多岐にわたっています。

本連載では、こうした課題のひとつひとつを、私たち DMLT チームのメンバーそれぞれの専門領域から掘り下げてご紹介します。技術的な内容だけでなく、実際のプロジェクトで直面しやすいポイントや、現実的なアプローチについても触れていく予定です。

 

DMLTとは

Data Management and Landscape Transformation — おそらく SAP の中でも長い部署名の一つではないでしょうか。以前は SLO（System Landscape Transformation）という名称でしたが、2015 年ごろに現在の名称に変わりました。グローバルのサービスチームで、年間300~400件のプロジェクト支援を行っています。

前半の Data Management は、お客様のデータに関する困り事を解決するコンサルティング窓口であり、後半の Landscape Transformation はご相談のみでなく、目指したい姿に向かってお客様のシステムを Transformation する、End to End のソリューションを提供するコンサルティングサービスとなります。

 

連載ラインナップ

Vol.1：M&A とデータマネジメント
M&Aや事業再編において、ビジネス継続と円滑な統合・分離を実現するには何が必要か。Carve-out、Day1対応、TSAなどの典型的な課題を整理します。

Vol.2：Autonomous Enterprise に備えるマスターデータ品質（運用）
自律型エンタープライズの実現に向けて、マスターデータの品質をどう維持・向上させるか。運用の観点から紹介します。

Vol.3：その“データ”、活かすための準備はできていますか？「Data Management Maturity Assessment」とは 
データや AI 活用に向けた、データの大切さ、設計・運用の難しさ、それを打破するきっかけとなる「Data Management Maturity Assessment」についてご紹介します。

Vol.4：Data Health Check と Data Volume Management
データ品質とデータ量の可視化を通じて、システム変革に向けた現状を把握。移行や最適化に向けて押さえるべき分析ポイントを解説します。

Vol.5：Information Lifecycle Management（ILM）
データの保管・アーカイブ・削除を体系的に管理する SAP ILM の全体像と活用方法を紹介します。

Vol.6：Data Privacy (1) — GDPR と PII への対応
欧州 GDPR および個人識別情報（PII）に対するコンプライアンス対応の考え方と、SAP における実践的なアプローチを解説します。

Vol.7：Data Privacy (2) — Masking Solution
開発・テスト環境における個人データ保護として、データマスキングをどう実装・運用するかを紹介します。

Vol.8：大容量システム移行やダウンタイム短縮とデータマネジメント
大容量のデータをより短いダウンタイムで移行する技術とともに、Clean Core 実現とデータボリューム削減を目指すお客様向けのテーラーメイド移行支援をご紹介します。

Vol.9：法規制やビジネス変化に伴う、基幹システムの会計領域を中心としたトランスフォーメーション
SAP 財務会計における主要なデータ構造と、変革プロジェクト時に押さえておくべき管理ポイントを解説します。

 

どなたに読んでいただきたいか

本連載は、特定の製品知識がなくても読み進められるよう、各回でできる限り背景から丁寧に説明します。以下のような方にとって参考になれば幸いです。

  • SAP システムを利用している、またはこれから導入・移行を検討している
  • データマネジメントの重要性は感じているが、何から手を付けてよいか分からない
  • M&A・S/4HANA 移行・クラウド移行などの変革プロジェクトを計画している
  • Business AI Platform でのデータマネジメントが知りたい

各回は独立した内容としてお読みいただけますが、連載全体を通じてデータマネジメントの全体像が見えてくる構成になっています。ぜひ各回の公開をお見逃しなく。

 

次回：Vol.1 - M&Aとデータマネジメント

急なビジネス要求に対し、SAP ERP の観点からどう備えるか。

entry.title: Monitoring of SAP HANA on IBM Power Virtual Servers in IBM Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction

This blog post is about monitoring SAP HANA on IBM Power Virtual Server in IBM Cloud.
As developer in the SAP monitoring part in IBM Cloud I focus on the real features.
Monitoring the health of IT services is a standard.


IBM Cloud offers advanced features on monitoring SAP HANA Databases:

Not only covers the monitoring the recent state and the historical metrics in a timeline,
as early-warning system it allows for measures to prevent failures beforehand.

  •  The dashboard panels can be edited to change or add metric sources and layout
  •  Alarms are available with selfdefined thresholds for all available metrics
  •  Analyse failures in reviewing the historical metrics
  •  Alert views offer statistics of critical selfdefined events within a timeline
  •  Notifications of alert events can be targeted to a wide range of types: email, SMS, pager, Slack notification channel, Microsoft Teams notification channel, Webhooks, OpsGenie

The monitored data allows to

  •  enhance system visibility and control
  •  improve resource utilization and optimization
  •  reduce downtime and increase overall efficiency

Three configuration options are available for monitoring SAP:

  1.  SAP monitoring is enabled with minimal effort as extensions in the catalog tiles 'Power Virtual Server with VPC landing zone' and 'Power Virtual Server for SAP HANA'.
    SAP monitoring is enabled with minimal effort as extensions in the catalog tiles
    Power Virtual Server with VPC landing zone - variation 'Standard Landscape Variation
    and Power Virtual Server for SAP HANA' - variation 'SAP S/4HANA or BW/4HANA
    In the standard version of the infrastructure landscape enable monitoring SAP (via the optional parameter 'enable_monitoring' set to true).
    This triggers the automated creation of an additional VSI for collecting and forwarding SAP metrics,
    and one IBM Cloud sysdig Instance will be created to show SAP metrics in UI Dashboards.
    Readme file - IBM Cloud Solution for Power Virtual Server with VPC Landing Zone Standard Landscape Variation
    The IBM catlog tile 'Power Virtual Server for SAP HANA' offers the input variable 'sap_monitoring_vars'
    to edit the monitoring configuration for the deployable SAP System:
    'config_override': true or false will override existing monitoring configuration numbers
    'sap_monitoring_nr: this number defines the unique monitoring configuration number, visible in the UI dashboard as part of the instance source name
    Readme file - IBM Cloud Catalog - Power Virtual Server for SAP HANA : 'SAP S/4HANA or SAP BW/4HANA

  2. The semi-automated configuration with Ansible roles is described here Monitoring SAP on Power with Ansible roles https://github.com/IBM/ansible-power-linux-sap/blob/main/roles/monitoring_sap/README.md .

  3. The manual configuration of SAP monitoring in IBM Cloud is rather focussing on the technical details and described here Getting started with IBM Cloud Monitoring for SAP systems. 

The metrics of SAP HANA DB are visualized in one dashboard.
Processes and instances are monitored in the SAP System dashbaord.
Up to 99 different SAP Systems can be configured for monitoring on one VSI,
The dashboards show the metrics separated per SAP System or merged into one dashboard.

Summary

IBM Cloud Monitoring for SAP Systems on IBM Power Virtual Server provides tailored dashboards and flexible configuration options to ensure optimal performance and reliability for mission-critical SAP workloads.
With automated, semi-automated, and manual setup choices, organizations can enhance system visibility, optimize resource utilization, and reduce downtime.
Dashboards deliver real-time insights into SAP S/4HANA databases and application metrics, enabling proactive management and improved operational efficiency.

entry.title: From Vision to Transformation: EDP's Strategic SAP Modernization Journey
entry.content:
1. Company Profile

EDP is a leading vertically integrated global energy company headquartered in Lisbon, Portugal, with operations spanning Europe, the Americas, and APAC. Established in 1976, combining power generation, transmission, distribution and retail supply of electricity and natural gas.

EDP’s diversified business is organized principally around three core segments:

  • Renewables — Development, ownership and operation of renewable energy assets including wind (onshore and offshore), solar, hydro and energy storage; administered partly through its major affiliate EDP Renováveis.
  • Networks — Transmission and distribution infrastructure providing reliable delivery of electricity across different markets, with extensive grid assets in key countries.
  • Client Solutions & Energy Management — Customer-facing energy supply, gas commercialization, smart solutions, and energy services for residential, commercial and industrial users.

With a workforce of around 12,000+ employees, EDP serves millions of electricity and gas customers globally and manages a substantial installed generation capacity. It is actively transforming its portfolio toward low-carbon sources, aiming for coal-free operations and a fully renewable mix by 2030, aligned with broader decarbonization and net-zero objectives.

The operating model, includes four business platforms and a set of global business enablement functions, organised in five global regions.

 As SAP Portugal's largest customer, EDP has maintained a strategic partnership with SAP for over 30 years. The company was also among the first organizations to adopt SAP for Utilities (IS-U) in the early 2000s, laying the foundation for a long-standing journey of innovation and digital transformation.

2. Customer Transformation context

Some years ago, EDP defined the future SAP strategy having in consideration the following decision drivers:

  • Instance unification ( Per geography, per capability, per platform)
  • Standardization ( Greenfield, Bluefield, Brownfield)
  • Hosting strategy ( Self Hosted, Mixed, RISE)
  • Integration and interoperability ( SAP proprietary, Mixed, Industry Standards)
  • Development and Change Management ( External microservices, BTP, side by side)

These drivers were classified on the impact and on cost/ROI. In the end, after EDP evaluation, the SAP future strategy for EDP was defined as a strategy with the SAP main pillars: RISE and BTP as part of their reference architecture.

In Q3 2024, their SAP transformation was initiated by signing the 1st RISE contract for the Client Solutions (Utilities Retail B2B and B2C), Q2 2025 an RISE contract for the Networks Spain ( Utilities Distribution) and Q4 2025 an additional RISE contract for their Utilities business in Brazil.

3. The Global Business Services (EDP Shared Services) Transformation

In parallel and as following stage for their transformation, EDP was evaluating the remaining business platforms and defined as part of the strategy to evolve the Global Business Services (GBS) platform by 2028. The GBS platform includes 2 different scopes and systems: SIM-F for Finance & Logistics and SIM-H for HR payroll processes (EDP has the majority of the SuccessFactors solutions).

 The GBS platform has the responsibility to EDP has a shared services company and supports the 1500 EDP’s companies in 24 countries.

In early 2023, SAP initiated a collaborative study by a VIS service (by the MaxAttention team) to assess possible paths for EDP to evolve the SIM-F that is currently in SAP ECC. That service allowed EDP to understand all the possible paths to evolve to S/4 and SAP recommendations on different dimensions such as migration, integration, custom code and best practices. The assessment was further strengthened by a comprehensive business case, developed collaboratively with EDP and supported by SAP Value Advisory, to quantify the expected business value and establish a robust foundation for decision-making.

EDP objective for this evolution was set clearly has “The move to SAP S/4HANA enables EDP to transform business processes through Fit to Standard, Clean Core and Efficiency while enabling innovation at scale for the future. As part of this initiative, EDP initiated an RFP to enable a global negotiation with SAP, encompassing the expanded EDP GBS scope, as well as the consolidation of SuccessFactors licensing, SAP services, SAP AI, SAP BTP and SAP BDC negotiations.

 The global negotiations were finalized and signed at the end of Q2 2026, enabling EDP to initiate the GBS project named EDGE.

4. Future Direction of EDP and the SAP Transformation Program

EDP's SAP transformation journey does not end here. As part of the broader EDP Global Business Services (GBS) platform strategy, EDP will continue to assess the adoption of SAP Central Finance and SAP Group Reporting to further enhance and standardize its financial management and consolidation processes.

 Looking ahead, the organization will evaluate another major transformation initiative: the modernization of its largest SAP landscape supporting electricity distribution operations in Portugal, including SAP for Utilities and Enterprise Asset Management solutions. This strategic program will be a key priority throughout 2026 and 2027, with activities focused on defining the transformation roadmap and preparing the Request for Proposal, which is expected to be launched in late 2027 or early 2028.

While significant milestones have already been achieved, this marks the beginning rather than the end of EDP's transformation journey. With a clear vision, strong executive sponsorship, and a commitment to continuous innovation.

entry.title: How to Connect Claude Code to ABAP with Zero-Config MCP
entry.content:
Before We Start - What This Post Deliberately Skips
- What is Claude Code, and what are the available models?
- What is MCP (Model Context Protocol) and how it works conceptually?
- What is vibe coding and why AI-assisted development matters?
- What are the benefits of using AI in SAP development?
- What development platforms are available (Web, Desktop, CLI)?
If you need more details, you can find these topics in sap community portal. Read that, then come back here.

What This Post Covers - and Why It Matters?

This post walks you through connecting Claude Code to a live SAP system using the ABAP Remote Filesystem extension's native MCP server - no external Node.js setup, no manual JSON editing, just a single toggle. 

What this changes for SAP development:

  • No more hand-typed routine code. TYPE declarations, SELECT statements, ALV setup - written directly into the SAP object from a single prompt.
  • No more AI guesswork. Without a live connection, AI tools hallucinate tables and function modules that don't exist. With MCP, every suggestion is grounded in your actual system.
  • No more setup overhead. What used to require a custom Node.js project now takes one checkbox and one command - open to any developer with VS Code.
  • Clean Core by default. Claude works through the same ADT API and released objects that Eclipse uses, so generated code fits your governance model instead of fighting it.

This is the point where AI-assisted ABAP stops being a chat-window experiment and starts being a real part of your IDE.

 
Goal: I will cover the entire topic in 4 parts:
|Part 1| Installing Claude Code on Windows and setting up the VS Code extension |
|Part 2| Connecting VS Code to your live SAP system via the ABAP FS extension |
|Part 3| Enabling the native MCP server with a single toggle - no Node.js setup, no JSON editing |
|Part 4| A live demo: one plain-English prompt creates a real, running ABAP report on SAP |
If you're here, you already know why this matters. Let's get to the actual steps.
 
Pre-requisite: This guide assumes you already have an active Claude subscription (Pro, Max, Team, or Enterprise). If not, sign up at claude.ai before proceeding
 
Part 1 - The Setup: Getting Claude Code Running on Windows
Step 1.1 - Install Claude Code CLI
Open PowerShell (the built-in Windows command-line shell) and run:

 

This downloads Anthropic's official installer and executes it in one step. 'irm' fetches the script, 'iex' runs it.
> ⚠️ Common Error - Git Bash Not Found
> Claude Code needs a bash shell to run on Windows. If you see this error, install Git for Windows from [git-scm.com/downloads/win](https://git-scm.com/downloads/win). During setup, select: Git from the command line and also from 3rd-party software. Then re-run the install command above.
Step 1.2 — Verify the Installation

 

Close PowerShell and open a fresh window. Run:
You should see a version number like 'claude 1.x.x'. If PowerShell says claude is not recognized, the PATH wasn't set automatically. Fix it manually:

 

1. Start → search Environment Variables in system settings
2. Edit the environment variables for your account
3. Select Path → Edit → New → paste ( in this case path can be taken from powershell commnad prompt)
 for eg: 

 

4. Close and reopen PowerShell, then re-run claude --version.

> Run claude doctor - this command checks your PATH, authentication, Node.js version, and config in one shot. Always the first step when something isn't working.
Step 1.3 - Launch Claude Code and the VS Code Extension
Navigate to your project folder and launch Claude Code.
The first launch opens your browser to authenticate with your Anthropic account (Pro, Max, Team, or Enterprise all work). 
For everyday ABAP work, the VS Code extension is where you'll spend most of your time.
Download and Install VS code from code.visualstudio.com for windows if you don't already have it. Once it is installed.Open VS Code, press 'Ctrl+Shift+X', search for Claude Code (published by Anthropic), and install it. Once installed, the Claude icon appears in the Activity Bar and connects automatically to your account.

 

The extension gives you a persistent chat panel inside VS Code - ask Claude about the file you have open, highlight any ABAP block and ask it to explain or refactor, and switch between ABAP and CAP projects in the same session. No terminal switching required.
> Note on Software Center installs: If your organization restricts installations, Claude Code for VS Code is typically available via your company Software Center / Company Portal.
 
Part 2 - Connecting VS Code to Your SAP System via ABAP FS
This is where the magic begins. The ABAP Remote Filesystem (ABAP FS)extension - built by Marcello Urbani - connects VS Code to your SAP system using the same ADT REST API that Eclipse uses. Once connected, your ABAP objects (programs, classes, CDS views, behavior definitions) appear as files in VS Code. Claude Code can then read, write, and review them like any local code file.
Pre-requisites Before You Connect
You'll need these four things from your SAP system before starting:
| Hostname or IP | The address of your SAP server (hostname preferred - IP causes SSL issues explained below) |
| ADT Service active | Go to transaction SICF → '/sap/bc/adt' must be activated |
| Client number| e.g. '100' - visible in the SAP logon screen |
|Username & Password| Your regular SAP development user |
| Port | HTTPS: '44300' (typical) | HTTP: '8000' for instance 00, '8001' for instance 01 |
 
Step 2.1 - Install and Configure ABAP FS
1. In VS Code Extensions ('Ctrl+Shift+X'), search ABAP remote filesystem by Marcello Urbani → Install.
2. Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → type ABAP FS: Connection Manager → open it.
3. Add a new connection and fill in the fields:
   - Name: anything you like (e.g. DEV or SANDBOX)
   - Server URL:'https://<hostname>:44300'
   - ⚠️ Do NOT add '/sap/bc/adt' to the URL - the extension adds this automatically. If you include it, the path doubles and the connection fails.
   - Client, Username, Language: 'EN'
4. Save.
5. Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → ABAP FS: Connect to an SAP system→ select your connection → enter password.
 

 

> ⚠️ SSL Certificate Error (very common on internal systems)
> If you see IP does not match certificate's altnames - this is because your SAP system's SSL certificate was issued with a hostname, not the IP. The fix: Get the hostname (e.g. 'https://sapdev.company.com:44300') and use that instead of the IP. 
> ⚠️ADT Service Not Found
> If you see Resource /sap/bc/adt does not exist - the ADT service isn't activated. Run tcode SICF → navigate to default_host → 'sap/bc/adt' → right-click → Activate Service.
 
Step 2.2 - Navigating ABAP Objects
ABAP FS gives you two ways to find objects. Object Search lets you find anything by name instantly. If you prefer a tree view like Eclipse ADT, enable SAP GUI Integration in your connection settings - this gives you a full package tree (Dictionary, Source Code Library, Transactions) and even embeds SAP GUI for HTML directly inside VS Code, so you can run transactions without leaving the editor.
1. Click OBJECT SEARCH in the left sidebar
2. Type the object or package name (e.g. 'ZCL_MY_CLASS' or 'Z_MY_PACKAGE')
3. Double-click any result to open it as a text file in VS Code
4. To edit: right-click  → edit → save (changes push back to SAP automatically)
Once an object is open in VS Code, open the integrated terminal (Ctrl+) and run claude. Some prompts that immediately pay off:

 

Part 3 -The Game Changer: Enabling the Native MCP Server

 

This is the step that changes everything - and the reason this post exists.
Why ABAP FS Alone Isn't Enough?
With only ABAP FS connected, Claude can see only the one file you currently have open. Ask it to create a new class and it might invent table names, function modules, or BAdI definitions that don't exist on your system. It's helpful, but it's guessing.
          ABAP FS Only                                         ABAP FS + MCP
Claude sees what we search Manually Claude autonomously searches any object on the system
Hallucinates objects it hasn't seen Grounded in live SAP system data
You copy-paste code manually Claude reads, writes, activates autonomously
No transport awareness Creates & assigns transports via ADT

 

The MCP (Model Context Protocol) server gives Claude Code a live connection to your SAP system's ADT API - the same backend Eclipse uses. Claude stops guessing and starts knowing.
Step 3.1 - Enable the MCP Server (One Toggle)
This is where the zero-config part comes in. You don't need to install anything extra, clone a Node.js repo, or edit JSON files manually.
Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → search ABAP FS MCP → select it → enable Autostart.
That's it. The ABAP FS extension spins up a local MCP server on your machine (default port 4847). You'll see a VS Code notification confirming it's running:
> ABAP MCP Server running on port 4847. External AI clients can connect to http://localhost:4847/mcp

Step 3.2 - Register the MCP Server with Claude Code
Now tell Claude Code where to find the ABAP tools. Run this in the VS Code integrated terminal:
This registers the local MCP server with Claude Code. The configuration is saved to your '.claude.json' file and persists across sessions - you only need to do this once.
Step 3.3 - Verify the Connection

 

open the terminal and run this command once the MCP server is added 
You should see:
Claude Code will confirm when the server is registered:
> abap-remote-fs has been added with HTTP transport at http://localhost:4847/mcp"*
The Complete Architecture
Claude Code can now autonomously:
- Search and read any ABAP object on the live SAP system
- write → syntax check → activate → unlock objects
- Create transport requests and assign objects
- Run ATC checks and read short dumps - all from a plain-English prompt
> Golden - In Auto mode, Claude will write, syntax check, and activate without pausing - that's the power of the agentic workflow. If you want to review before anything is activated, switch to Plan mode  shift+tab to cycle modes)  Claude will show you the full plan and wait for your approval. For production-adjacent systems, always run in Plan or Step mode.
Part 4 - Live Demo: One Prompt, One Real ABAP Program on SAP
Disclaimer: This is my first time setting up all this and explore, so i just started with a very simple scenario. Going forward will create RAP objects and other complex artifacts and see the efficiency.
Let me show you what all of this enables. Here's the exact prompt I used:
> Create a new ABAP executable program ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01 in my ZVS_DEMO package. Fetch data from MARA for a user-specified Material Number (select-option) and display Material Number, Type, and Created By in an ALV grid. Write in ABAP 7.5 syntax.
One prompt. No templates. No copy-paste. Here's what Claude did.
Act 1 - Claude Plans Before It Codes
Claude didn't immediately start writing code. It:
1. Confirmed the target package ('ZVS_DEMO') exists on the system
2. Detected that the program creation triggered a **transport request dialog** — and recognised it was the likely source of a cancellation
3. Called the SAP ADT API via Bash to **list existing open transports**
4. Selected the correct transport and retried the creation
There was a little bit delay on my end to confirm the transport. and it gave me this message.
> Package confirmed. The cancellation is likely a transport request dialog. Let me check for existing transports, then retry.
This is the part that impresses ABAP developers most. Claude handled a real SAP workflow quirk .
Act 2 - The Program Appears on SAP
Claude then created the program on the live system. You can watch its autonomous todo list mid-execution:
- Create ABAP program ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01 in ZVS_DEMO package via MCP
- Get workspace URI for the new program
- Write ABAP source code into the program
- Activate the program and check diagnostics
'ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01' appeared in the VS Code Explorer sidebar - a real, registered ABAP program on the SAP system, not a local file.
You can see initially there was no program created in my package 
After few seconds, the program is created 

 

Act 3 - Full ABAP 7.5 Source Code Written
With the object created, Claude fetched the workspace URI and wrote the complete source code directly into the SAP program. What it generated:
No placeholder code. Real, working ABAP in the correct syntax release, referencing actual MARA field types from the live system.
Act 4 - Running the Program
After activation, there are two ways to run it:
Option A - Classic SAP GUI:Log in to SAP GUI →run the program → Execute
Option B - Embedded SAP Web GUI(what I used): VS Code can open the SAP Web GUI directly in a browser tab, embedded inside the IDE. No separate login or window switching required.
Enter selection criteria:
The selection screen appeared with the Matnr  field exactly as specified. I entered a range of 11to 21. The value help (F4) shows the available materials in the system
Material Number, Material Type, and Created By - exactly what was asked for. Live data from MARA. Report running.
 
Summary:
|Claude understands SAP context| It identified the transport dialog, queried existing transports via ADT, and handled a real SAP workflow quirk autonomously 
| It created a real SAP object| 'ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01' exists on the live system, created via ADT REST API through MCP - not a file on disk 
| Production-quality ABAP 7.5 | TYPE declarations referencing actual MARA field types, correct naming conventions, modern SQL syntax 
| Minutes, not hours| The developer's job shifts to reviewing - not writing skeletons, hunting transport dialogs, or looking up field types 
| Fully iterative| Once created, you can ask Claude to add authority checks, error handling, ALV field catalogues, or additional filters - each iteration in seconds 
| You stay in control | Claude's todo list is transparent - you see every step it plans. You review the code. You decide what activates 
 
Where to Go from Here?
Here are five things to try once you have the setup working:
1. Understand inherited code - Explain the purpose and flow of report Z_*_REPORT. Include tables accessed, authority checks, deprecated patterns like SELECT .
2. Generate a CDS view - Create a CDS view hierarchy for Sales Order analysis: interface view joining VBAK+VBAP, projection view with Fiori Elements. UI annotations for a List Report.
3. Build a Travel Request POC -  RAP Business Object with CRUD, approve/reject action, draft handling, OData V4 binding, Fiori Elements UI.
4. Write ABAP Unit tests - Generate a unit test class 
5. Code review and modernization - Review this class for Clean ABAP violations, missing authority checks, SELECT * usage, and SELECT inside LOOP. Propose S/4-equivalent replacements.
 
The bottom line: The integration that used to require manual Node.js setup, JSON editing, and external package management now takes a single Ctrl+Shift+P toggle and one terminal command.
 
The ABAP Remote Filesystem team has done the hard work. Claude Code does the coding. You do the review.
Give it a try - and let me know in the comments what use case you tackle first.
 
A personal note  : This is my first time diving deeper into vibe coding and MCP concepts. I come from a classic ABAP background, and everything covered here -  from the VS Code setup to the MCP server to the live demo - I explored and built from scratch over the past few weeks. The scenario is intentionally simple. The goal was to get the fundamentals right, document them honestly, and share what works. There is a lot more ground to cover - RAP generation at scale, Clean Core extensibility patterns, ATC pre-checks via Claude Code, and deeper agentic workflows. I'll be going further in the posts ahead. If you're also just getting started with this, I hope this gives you a solid, working foundation to build on. And if you're already further ahead - I'd love to hear what you've learnt in the comments.
 
Note: All screenshots in this post are taken from a live S/4HANA development system. Code generated by Claude Code should always be reviewed and tested in a sandbox environment before activation in any productive system.

entry.title: SAP Fiori Tools 2607 Update: Skills, Fullstack AI Development, and Performance
entry.content:
The 2607 release of SAP Fiori tools focuses on AI-driven development, but there is also good news for the human tooling: faster startup, more Page Editor capabilities, and a few quality-of-life fixes. Here is what is new.

AI-Driven Development with the MCP Server for SAP Fiori

MCP support in SAP Business Application Studio

SAP Business Application Studio now includes an AI Chat capability that you can use for MCP-supported AI-driven SAP Fiori development directly from the browser. No installation is needed: the MCP server for SAP Fiori comes pre-installed in SAP Fiori and Fullstack Dev spaces, alongside the UI5 and CAP MCP servers.

Installing the MCP server locally

For local development, the MCP server for SAP Fiori is now available through the MCP registry on GitHub. You can also install it as a Claude plugin, together with our new skills (more on that below).

By the way, if you have the SAP Fiori tools extension pack installed in VS Code, you automatically have a version of the MCP server for SAP Fiori available that can be consumed by the GitHub Copilot extension.

Skills: the next layer on top of MCP

MCP servers provide the tools an AI agent can call, but tools alone do not tell the agent how to approach a complex, multi-step development job. That is the job of a skill. A skill bundles domain knowledge, step-by-step guidance, and best practices into a reusable prompt-based workflow that directs the AI through a specific task. Instead of leaving the model to figure out the right sequence of tool calls on its own, a skill encodes that knowledge explicitly, leading to more reliable and consistent results.

With this release, the SAP Fiori tools team publishes the first set of skills that complement the MCP server for SAP Fiori:

SkillWhat it does
sap-fiori-app-developmentGuides the creation and modification of SAP Fiori elements applications in CAP and standalone projects
sap-fiori-analytical-chartAdds an analytical chart and table hybrid to a List Report, supporting CAP and ABAP RAP (OData V4)
sap-fiori-add-visual-filterAdds chart-based visual filters to the filter bar or value help in SAP Fiori elements applications
sap-fiori-create-cliRuns and automates the @​sap-ux/create CLI for generating, converting, and managing Fiori projects
sap-fiori-eslint-pluginConfigures, migrates, or runs ESLint with the @​sap-ux/eslint-plugin-fiori-tools in SAP Fiori projects

The easiest way to get the skills is through the Claude plugin, where they are included alongside the MCP server for SAP Fiori. Alternatively, you can install individual skills via skills.sh, an open ecosystem for reusable agent skills that supports Cursor, GitHub Copilot, and others.

We will continuously expand and improve this set of skills, and we are very much looking forward to your feedback.

Fullstack development in VS Code

The MCP server for SAP Fiori pairs with the new ABAP MCP server, which is part of the ABAP development tools for Visual Studio Code. Together, the two bridge the gap between backend ABAP services and the SAP Fiori frontend, enabling a true fullstack AI-driven development experience from within VS Code. For a concrete walkthrough of how this can look in practice, I recommend watching my ABAPConf presentation.

A new top-level tool structure

Based on feedback we have collected from usage, we restructured the top-level tools of the MCP server for SAP Fiori. More capabilities are now surfaced directly at the top level, in particular the app generators and metadata retrieval, which are needed to properly connect backend services to the frontend layer. This restructuring is available as of version 1.8.1 of the MCP server for SAP Fiori.

The top-level tools now are:

ToolWhat it does
search_docsSearches SAP Fiori elements, annotations, UI5, OPA5, and SAP Fiori tools documentation
list_fiori_appsScans a directory to find existing SAP Fiori applications
list_sap_systemsLists SAP systems stored in the user's environment
download_odata_service_metadataDownloads the EDMX metadata of an OData service from an SAP system
generate_fiori_app_odataGenerates a new SAP Fiori application for OData (non-CAP) projects, for example RAP-based services
generate_fiori_app_capGenerates a new SAP Fiori application inside an existing CAP project
list_functionality (Step 1 of 3)Lists supported modification operations for an existing SAP Fiori application
get_functionality_details (Step 2 of 3)Returns the required parameters for a chosen modification
execute_functionality (Step 3 of 3)Executes the modification with the provided parameters

This structure also sets the stage for what comes next. We are working on adaptation project support for the MCP server for SAP Fiori, which will add tools for creating and modifying adaptation projects directly through AI agents. They will join the top-level structure shown above.

Renovations and Performance Improvements

Moving from CommonJS to ECMAScript Modules

ECMAScript Modules (ESM) are the official JavaScript module standard, supported natively in modern browsers and Node.js. With this release, the SAP Fiori tools suite and its Node.js modules migrate from CommonJS to ESM. For us, this means better ecosystem compatibility, tree-shaking for smaller bundles (unused code eliminated at build time), and more efficient asynchronous module loading.

ESLint 10 upgrade

The 2603 release delivered ESLint 9 support for the tools and generator. With 2607 we take the next step and upgrade to ESLint 10, keeping the toolchain current and continuing the move toward the modern flat config format.

80% faster extension startup

As the SAP Fiori tools extensions have grown over time, startup time had crept up correspondingly. Through targeted refactoring and more efficient bundling, loading only what is necessary at startup, we reduced the startup time of the SAP Fiori tools extensions by around 80%.

Other Improvements

A few smaller additions that are worth calling out:

  • Fiori Generator error details: Clicking on an error in the generator now opens the full error details in the console, making it much easier to diagnose what went wrong.
  • Page Editor building blocks: The Page Editor now supports Page, Form, and Field building blocks in custom pages and custom sections, rounding out the building block coverage for custom development.
  • SAP Fiori elements enhancements via Page Editor: The Page Editor now lets you configure a notes reuse component, inline edit, mass edit, and creation dialogs (and more) for SAP Fiori elements applications, all through the graphical configuration interface without touching annotations manually.
  • Reuse existing LineItem annotations for tables: When adding a table, you can now select an existing LineItem annotation from the service instead of always having to create a new one. This avoids duplicate annotations and makes it easier to build UIs on top of well-annotated services.
  • Unique control IDs in XML generation: The Page Editor now correctly generates unique control IDs when producing XML for custom elements.
  • Download UI5 ABAP repository content from the public cloud: You can now download source artifacts directly from a deployed SAPUI5 ABAP repository, so you can continue local development even when the source files are not available. This closes a gap for public cloud customers, who previously had no way to import from the ABAP backend. See the SAP Help Portal documentation for more information.

Staying Up to Date

If you want to track changes in SAP Fiori tools between release blog posts, the What's New section in the SAP Help Portal is the best place to look. It is updated continuously with newly delivered capabilities and fixes.

For documentation and sample code covering all building blocks available when creating SAP Fiori elements applications, visit the SAP Fiori development portal.

Questions and feedback? Leave a comment below or reach out through the community.
